summary: George Hotz expresses frustration with the current state of laptops, arguing that Apple's quality is declining while Linux alternatives like Asahi fail due to Apple's closed hardware. He critiques Framework's focus on upgradability as a misguided tradeoff that sacrifices build quality and polish. He proposes a hypothetical 'hackbook' - a single-configuration, no-compromise Linux laptop built on AMD's Strix Halo with obsessive attention to polish and simple yearly iteration. The post concludes with him hoping someone else will build this so he doesn't have to, acknowledging he's bikeshedding rather than committing to the project.
key_points: Apple's increasing use of closed-source microcontrollers makes Linux ports like Asahi fundamentally limited - they'll never achieve full polish
Framework's modular philosophy trades away weight, rigidity, and polish for upgradability that power users don't actually need
Consumer electronics naming should be dead simple: good product, simple name, increment the number each year
The best developers are what make Apple's ecosystem work - if they leave, Apple loses its secret advantage
Engineering is about tradeoffs, and the right tradeoff for a developer laptop is zero customization in exchange for maximum polish
money_quotes: My dream laptop is simple, a MacBook with Linux, supported by a company that is user aligned.
Like jailbreaking, it may start off strong when people are excited, but support for the next generation and that last bit of polish won't ever get there.
I don't care one bit about upgradability or customizability. After a year or two, I'm happy to throw it out and buy a new one.
Engineering is all about trade-offs, and the trade-off I'd prefer is 0 upgradability or customizability in exchange for less weight and more polish.
Consumer electronics naming is very simple. Make a good product with a simple name.

Apple will not understand what they had until they lose it, the only reason anything works on Mac at all is because there's 100,000 amazing developers who use these machines every day; they put some work into making their house nice.
For some reason people say I get distracted, but comma has been around for ten years following this playbook.
Can somebody please build a good MacBook replacement and make it a Schelling point everyone will switch to so I don't have to think about this anymore?
key_insight: The laptop market is failing power users because companies optimize for the wrong tradeoffs - modularity over polish, product line complexity over simplicity - leaving a gap for a user-aligned company to build one excellent, no-compromise Linux machine.
